coredata.dc:title: Rapid microwave preparation of nitrogen doped carbon dots and their applications as highly sensitive nanoprobe for hematin
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Tianze
Dong, Yuanyuan
Yan, Qiuyan
Liu, Zixin
Zeng, Hong
coredata.dc:description: In this work, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) were obtained by microwave rapid preparation method with lipase and guanidine hydrochloride as precursors in only 6 min. In order to achieve high sensitivity detection of hematin, based on obtained NCDs as a nanoprobe, a new detection method based on photoluminescence (PL) change was developed. Further studies have shown that the sensing strategy relied on the inner filter effect to selectively quench the PL emission of NCDs at 379 nm by hematin under 320 nm excitation. The nanoprobe had a wide linear range (0.2–13 μmol/L) and a detection limit of 0.07 μmol/L. The method was effectively applied to the identification of hematin in human blood samples. The proposed nanoprobe has the characteristics of simple and efficient design. It can achieve rapid and accurate detection, which is very suitable for real-time analysis of blood samples to facilitate diagnosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Microwave
Nitrogen doped
Hematin
Inner filter effect

Structure and composition of the NCDs
The quenching mechanism for detecting hematin

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of NCDs, the inset of (A): high-resolution TEM image of NCDs; (B) Size distribution plots of NCDs.
(A) FT-IR spectra of NCDs; high-resolution XPS spectra of (B) C 1 s, (C) N 1 s, and (D) O 1 s for NCDs.
(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of NCDs; (B) 3D PL spectra of NCDs; (C) 3D-fluorescence topographical map of NCDs; (D) CIE color coordinates of NCDs.

(A) PL spectra of NCDs with different concentrations of hematin (0.0–100.0 μmol/L); (B) The relationship between (F0-F)/F0 and hematin concentration; (C) The linear correlation plot in the hematin concentration ranging from 0.2 to 13.0 μmol/L. (D) PL response of NCDs toward various interfering substances. (hemoglobin (Hb), vitamin c (Vc), glutathione (GSH), cysteine (Cys), glycine (Gly), arginine (Arg) and glutamate (Glu)).
(A) The UV absorption spectra of hematin and PL spectra of NCDs; (B) PL lifetime decay profiles of NCDs in the absence and presence of hematin.
Schematic representation of the preparation and the sensing applications of NCDs.
An overview on recently reported methods for determination of hematin.
Results for the determination of hematin in real samples.

head.ce:title: Rapid microwave preparation of nitrogen doped carbon dots and their applications as highly sensitive nanoprobe for hematin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Heilongjiang Institute of Technology, Harbin 150050, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Heilongjiang Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials and Chemical Engineering, Heilongjiang Institute of Technology, Harbin 150050, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Chemical Engineering, Harbin Engineering University, Harbin 150001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Chemical Engineering, Harbin Engineering University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials Science and Chemical Engineering, Harbin Engineering University, Harbin 150001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) were obtained by microwave rapid preparation method with lipase and guanidine hydrochloride as precursors in only 6 min. In order to achieve high sensitivity detection of hematin, based on obtained NCDs as a nanoprobe, a new detection method based on photoluminescence (PL) change was developed. Further studies have shown that the sensing strategy relied on the inner filter effect to selectively quench the PL emission of NCDs at 379 nm by hematin under 320 nm excitation. The nanoprobe had a wide linear range (0.2–13 μmol/L) and a detection limit of 0.07 μmol/L. The method was effectively applied to the identification of hematin in human blood samples. The proposed nanoprobe has the characteristics of simple and efficient design. It can achieve rapid and accurate detection, which is very suitable for real-time analysis of blood samples to facilitate diagnosis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a new type of zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials in the scale of 2–10 nm with outstanding optical properties [1–3]. CDs have abundant functional groups on the surface, giving them good water solubility. In addition, the strong photobleaching resistance of CDs makes them very suitable for sensing, cell imaging and targeted cancer therapy [2,4–9]. As a novel nanoprobe, CDs are superior to inorganic semiconductor quantum dots and organic dyes [10–13]. In the past few years, many methods have been successfully used to prepare CDs, including hydrothermal method, microwave method, ultrasonic assisted method and electrochemical method [14–16]. Among these methods, microwave method is widely used in the preparation of CDs due to its advantages of simplicity, rapidity and environmental friendliness. After a detailed analysis of the structure and properties of CDs, it is found that the introduction of heteroatoms into the structure of CDs can produce defects in the carbon skeleton and adjust their PL properties [17]. Thus, a variety of metal and non-metal elements have been developed for doping CDs to regular their performance [18]. Among them, N is a typical dopant because it has five valence electrons, and the atomic size of N is comparable to that of C. Therefore, N doping is the most widely used method to improve the PL performance of CDs. N-CDs have shown excellent performance in biomedical applications such as bioimaging and biosensing. For example, Zhao et al. reported that N-CDs were synthesized via hydrothermal treatment of melamine and polyethyleneimine. The prepared NCDs exhibited excellent photostability, high quantum yield (26 %), and good water solubility. Furthermore, theses N-CDs were utilized for the detection of Fe3+ [19]. In addition, Halogen (F, Cl, Br and I) doping is also considered as an effective strategy to significantly improve the optical properties of CDs [20]. In our previous study, we prepared two types of o-phenylenediamine-derived CDs (FCDs1 and FCDs2) with different amounts of F at room temperature. It could be observed that PL emission of two prepared CDs had a significant red-shift due to the narrowing of the band gap caused by F doping [4]. Furthermore, N, S, P, Mn, Cu and many other metals and non-metals have been used to dope CDs [20].

Hematin (Fe3+ protoporphyrin IX liganded to OH-) represents the oxidized form of free heme, which is the prosthetic group of hemoglobin and contains iron. It plays a key role in various physiological processes, including oxygen, electron transfer, and drug metabolism [21–23]. The standard method is used to determine the concentration value of hematin in the range of 10–40 μmol/L for healthy red blood cell [24]. However, the concentration of hematin in disease cells is much higher than that found in healthy red blood cells. Jia et al. reported that the concentration of hematin in sickle cells was 2.3 times that of healthy red blood cells [25]. Liu et al. also reported an adsorption spectroscopy method to measure the concentration of hematin in sickle cells and healthy red blood cells, and found that the concentration of hematin in sickle cells was 3–5 times higher than that in healthy red blood cells [26]. In addition, excessive hematin can produce toxicity in the body, rupture red blood cells and other cells, produce harmful free radicals, resulting in lipid peroxidation [27,28]. Moreover, elevated hematin levels in the body can cause inflammatory conditions, including vascular diseases, renal failure, and immune-related disorders [27,29]. Although several methods such as high-performance liquid chromatography, mass spectrometry, absorption spectrophotometry, and electrochemical methods have been established for the detection of hematin, they are time-consuming, expensive, and not very sensitive [29]. Thus, it is of great significance to establish a simple, rapid and economical method for the detection of hematin.

In this study, a one-step microwave method was used to obtain N-doped CDs (NCDs) with blue emission selecting lipase and guanidine hydrochloride (GuHCl) as carbon sources and nitrogen sources. The yield of NCDs was found to be approximately 15 % by weight, and their quantum yield was determined to be 12.7 %. PL emission spectra of the prepared NCDs was found to have a maximum peak at 379 nm, which had obvious overlap with the absorption peak of hematin at 391 nm. It caused PL emission of NCDs to be significantly quenched in the presence of hematin. The phenomenon was used to develop a sensitive and selective PL sensing method for the detection of hematin from 0.2 to 13 μmol/L with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.07 μmol/L. The PL quenching mechanism was confirmed to be IFE. Furthermore, NCDs displayed stability even with the addition of some metal ions and small biological molecules. This stability, as well as the sensitivity and selectivity of PL sensing method, indicated that NCDs were a promising material in biosensing applications. In addition, the developed PL method was successfully applied to determine hematin in actual samples with satisfactory spiking recoveries. The preparation process and sensing application of NCDs are shown in Scheme 1, which indicates the preparation of NCDs and their applications for the detection of hematin.
Details are provided in the Supporting Information.

NCDs can be prepared through a simple one-step microwave method preparation. In a typical protocol, a mixture of 0.1 g of lipase and 6 g of GuHCl was prepared with 10 mL of deionized water. The above solution was then subjected to ultrasonic treatment until these components are completely dissolved to form a uniform mixture. Subsequently, the obtained solution was heated in a 700 W microwave oven for 6 min to form brown crude NCDs. After the solution was cooled to room temperature, 10 mL deionized water was added, and the crude NCDs were ultrasonically dissolved to form yellow solution. The resulting yellow solution was then separated using centrifugation at a speed of 10,000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was then filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane, which effectively removed some large particles from the mixture. After that, the liquid mixture was passed through a cellulose ester membrane with a molecular weight cutoff (MWCO) of 500–1000 for 48 h. This process allowed the removal of any unreacted precursors. Finally, the resulting solution was collected and subjected to freeze-drying in order to obtain high-quality NCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL assay of hematin with NCDs
ce:section.ce:para: To measure hematin using NCDs as the PL probe, follow this procedure. The hematin stock solution was diluted 10 times with a pH 7.0 Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer solution. Subsequently, 1.0 mL of hematin solution with varying concentrations was added to 1.0 mL of NCDs solution (15.0 μg/mL, prepared in BR buffer solution). Finally, PL spectra were examined by exciting at 320 nm. In order to assess the selectivity of the system, additional species were introduced to NCDs solution under the same parameters.

This method was used to identify the presence of hematin in healthy human blood samples. 4 mL blood sample was carefully mixed with 45 mL of isotonic 0.9 % NaCl solution. Subsequently, it was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 min. After centrifugation, the supernatant was poured out, and the red blood cells were washed three times to remove any remaining impurities. Then, the washed red blood cells were suspended in 50 mL of deionized water to release hematin. Finally, the mixture was centrifuged, and the hemolysate was concentrated and stored at a cool temperature of 5 °C for further analysis. Before detection, the hemolysate was diluted using ultrapure water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and composition of the NCDs
ce:section.ce:para: In this experiment, NCDs were prepared through microwave method using lipase and GuHCl as precursors. While small molecule carbon sources have been previously used to prepare CDs, the process of their formation remains speculative. It is believed to involve four stages, namely dehydration, polymerization, carbonization, and passivation [30]. Based on the process described above, the mechanism of how CDs are formed by using lipase as a carbon source is as follows. During the initial stages of microwave heating, the intermolecular dehydration occurs between lipase and GuHCl. This causes the molecules to become more concentrated and leads to the formation of new chemical bonds. As heating continues, the lipase and GuHCl undergo intramolecular polymerization and carbonization, forming CC and CN bonds within the carbon network, further generating the aromatic ring framework of NCDs. With the increase of heating time, the proportion of aromatic structure in the local area increases. When the aromatized structure reaches the critical supersaturation point, the nucleation explosive growth leads to the formation of NCDs. Subsequently, through the dispersion of aromatic groups, the surfaces of NCDs are passivated by various functional groups such as -OH, -COOH and -NH3, which reduce the reactivity of NCDs. The functional groups on the surface of CDs can usually help them to have higher stability in different ionic strength environments, which is essential for their use in various applications [31]. During the preparation of NCDs, a small amount of precursor molecules may be excessively polymerized, which leads to the formation of larger particles. In order to prevent this phenomenon, it is necessary to centrifuge prior to further purification steps. The centrifugation process is beneficial to remove the larger particles that have been formed, thereby obtaining a more purified sample. However, it is also possible that a small amount of precursor molecules is still not involved in the reaction during the preparation process. These molecules also contribute to the impurities in the final product. In order to solve this problem, dialysis treatment is required to remove any unreacted precursor molecules in the sample. The obtained sample has high purity and is suitable for further analysis or use in various applications. After the purification process, the yield of NCDs is calculated to be 15 wt%, and their quantum yield is calculated to be 12.7 % with quinine sulfate as a reference.

In order to understand the structure of the prepared NCDs, various analytical testing techniques are used. One of the effective methods is transmission electron microscopy (TEM). By analyzing the images produced by TEM, valuable insights about the structural properties of NCDs can be obtained. TEM image in Fig. 1 A shows that NCDs have good dispersibility. Furthermore, Fig. 1B reveals that NCDs exhibit a relatively narrow size distribution from 2.5 to 4.5 nm. It is worth noting that the prepared NCDs are spherical nanoparticles with an average size of 3.5 nm, which is similar to previously reported findings on CDs [32–34]. These results suggest that the preparation method used in this study is successful.

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) test is a widely used technique to determine the surface state and composition of CDs. After careful analysis of the FT-IR spectra of NCDs obtained in Fig. 2 A, some significant stretching vibrations of their different functional groups are revealed. The stretching vibrations of O-H and N-H functional groups occur in the range of 3400–3100 cm−1. These functional groups are ubiquitous in CDs [5]. In addition, the stretching vibration of CN can be observed at 1656 cm−1, indicating the presence of imine functional groups in NCDs. The stretching vibration of CC occurs at 1500 cm−1, demonstrating the presence of aromatic functional groups, which are common in CDs [35,36]. Furthermore, the C-N stretching vibration is observed at 1357 cm−1, confirming the presence of amine functional groups in NCDs. Finally, the C-O stretching vibration is observed at 1153 cm−1, which is attributed to the ether functional groups on NCDs. The above results indicate that there are a variety of N-containing groups in the NCDs structure. In addition, it also contains the formation of aromatic structures, which are common in reported CDs [35].

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum is an analytical technique used to investigate the elemental composition of CDs. XPS analysis of NCDs show that there are three peaks located approximately at C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s, respectively, as shown in Fig. S1. These peaks correspond to the carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen atoms present in NCDs. Additionally, the high-resolution XPS spectra show that the C 1 s band can be separated into four binding energies, corresponding to different chemical bonds. The first peak at 284.3 eV corresponds to CC/C−C bonds, while the second peak at 285.0 eV corresponds to the C−N bond. The third peak at 286.0 eV corresponds to the C−O bond, and the fourth peak at 288.0 eV corresponds to CN/CO bonds [37–39]. As shown in Fig. 2 C, N 1 s band has four peaks, including 398.3, 399.2, 400.1 and 401.0 eV, which are attributed to pyridinic N, amino N, pyrrolic N and graphitic N, respectively [40]. In Fig. 2D, the O 1 s band consists of two peaks, which are 531.0 eV of CO and 533.0 eV of C-O, respectively [4,41]. The presence of -COOH and -OH groups in NCDs indicates that they have high solubility in water. Among them, the -OH group provides hydrogen bonding sites that promote water solubility, while the -COOH group contributes to the overall charge distribution of the molecule, which helps to stabilize the molecule in the solution and enhance their solubility. In summary, the water solubility of NCDs is an important feature, which makes it widely used in drug delivery, fluorescent dyes and environmental detection.

In order to fully understand the optical properties of NCDs, two analytical techniques are used. The ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectrum is used to study the interaction between NCDs and light in the UV and vis regions of the electromagnetic spectrum. The PL spectrum is used to study the ability of NCDs to emit under light excitation. By combining the information obtained by these two analytical techniques, we can have a deeper understanding of the optical properties of NCDs.

The UV–vis absorption spectrum of NCDs is displayed in Fig. 3 A, which illustrates two primary absorption peaks: a strong peak at 207 nm caused by the π–π* transition of CC bonds, and a smaller peak at 283 nm attributed to the n–π* transition of CO on the surface of NCDs [36]. Figs. 3B and 3 C are the 3D PL spectra and 3D-fluorescence topography map of NCDs. NCDs are excited in the range of 260–450 nm. When the excitation wavelength is 320 nm, the maximum PL intensity is located at 379 nm. As shown in Fig. 3D, based on PL emission of NCDs, the color coordinates are calculated in the blue region (0.1599, 0.0914).

The quantum yield of NCDs is calculated to be approximately 12.7 %, with quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.1 mol/L H2SO4 used as the reference. The practical application of CDs-based probes requires them to have good PL stability. The stability of the PL intensity of NCDs is investigated under a series of ionic strengths, and PL intensity of NCDs show no significant decrease in 0–5 mol/L NaCl solutions with different concentrations, as shown in Fig. S2. Furthermore, the NCDs stored under UV light for 6 h are tested for PL intensity, and it is observed that the PL intensity decreased by 2 % during the remaining 6 h of irradiation, as shown in Fig. S3. It is found that NCDs display excellent stability after 7-day storage at room temperature, as shown in Fig. S4. These results suggest that NCDs have good stability, making them suitable for use in complex systems. Fig. S5 shows that PL intensity of NCDs remains almost constant in the pH range of 6.0–8.5, where the PL intensity is maximum at pH 7. Therefore, neutral pH 7 is selected as the best application condition.

Hematin is an effective quencher for PL emission of NCDs, which presents an opportunity to develop a PL nanoprobe for hematin detection using the turn-off method. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the effect of hematin concentration in the range of 0.0–100.0 μmol/L on the PL intensity of NCDs is demonstrated. The PL intensity of NCDs-based nanoprobes at around 379 nm gradually decrease with the increase of hematin concentration in the system. This indicates that the nanoprobes are sensitive to the concentration of hematin. Furthermore, the relationship between the concentration of hematin and the quenching PL intensity of NCDs is established to enable a quantitative test for the presence of hematin. As is shown in Fig. 4B, and a fine linear correlation occurs over an appropriate concentration range of hematin (0.2–13.0 μmol/L), with a linear regression: (F0-F)/F0=0.028C-0.0071 with R2 of 0.9956 (Fig. 4 C). The LOD (3S/N) of this method is estimated to be 0.07 μmol/L, which is considered satisfactory when compared to the hematin detection performance shown in Table 1. The main parameter for evaluating probe specificity is selectivity. Therefore, in this study the effects of various interfering substances for the detection results of hematin are investigated, including different biomolecules, metal ions, and anions that may be present in complex biological samples. In Fig. 4D, the PL intensity at 379 nm is recorded after adding different interfering substances to the NCDs solution with 320 nm as the excitation wavelength. The results show that the addition of biomolecules, metal ions, and anions do not significantly affect the PL intensity of NCDs, thus confirming the high selectivity of the method in detecting hematin.

In order to evaluate the effectiveness of the nanoprobe based on NCDs, hemolysate is used as a real sample. After the hemolysate samples are tested, and the spike-and-recovery method is used in this study by spiking the processed samples with known amounts of hematin standard solutions (1.0, 5.0 and 10.0 μmol/L). The results of this experiment are shown in Table 2, indicating that the recovery rate of the added sample is between 99.0 % and 103.6 %. This proves that the developed nanoprobe can accurately detect hematin in real samples. Furthermore, the relative standard deviation (RSD) is less than or equal to 3.1 %, which is the standard to measure the accuracy of the nanoprobe, which means that the results obtained by the nanoprobe are reliable and suitable for practical application. In summary, the findings of this study confirm that the developed nanoprobe is effective for the detection of hematin in hemolysate samples and has the potential for medical and diagnostic applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The quenching mechanism for detecting hematin
ce:section.ce:para: IFE is a phenomenon that occurs when the excitation or emission spectrum of the fluorophore overlaps with the absorption spectrum of the light absorber. This overlap leads to a decrease in the PL intensity of the fluorophore, and the decrease in PL intensity is directly related to the amount of light absorber content and exhibits a linear correlation within a specific range [4,42,47,48]. Therefore, IFE is developed as an effective analysis method for quantitative analysis. Generally, there is a necessary relationship between the PL quenching process of CDs by hematin and the IFE [23,44]. To confirm that the quenching mechanism of NCDs by hematin is IFE, a series of tests are performed, including UV absorption spectra and PL spectra.

In Fig. 5 A, the PL spectra of NCDs and the UV absorption spectra of hematin overlap entirely, which is a crucial factor for fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and IFE to take place. FRET and IFE are two distinct quenching mechanisms commonly observed in PL spectroscopy. FRET involves non-radiative energy transfer between a donor fluorophore and an acceptor fluorophore in proximity, leading to a decrease in donor fluorescence and an increase in acceptor fluorescence. On the other hand, the IFE occurs when the excitation or emission of the fluorophore is absorbed or scattered by a quencher present in the sample, leading to a reduction in the observed PL signal. Despite their differences, both FRET and IFE rely on the spectral overlap between the emission spectrum of the donor fluorophore and the absorption spectrum of the acceptor fluorophore (in the case of FRET) or the absorbance spectrum of the quencher (in the case of IFE). In Fig. 5B, the PL lifetime of NCDs is approximately 1.18 ns. Even after adding hematin, there is little to no change in the PL lifetime of NCDs, which remains at 1.15 ns. This suggests that the quenching mechanism affecting NCDs cannot be attributed to FRET, as that process can alter the PL lifetime. Therefore,

the overlap between PL spectra of NCDs and UV–vis absorption spectra of hematin indicates that IFE is a possible quenching mechanism that leads to the decrease of the PL intensity of NCDs in the presence of hematin. This is because IFE does not affect the lifetime of the fluorophore, unlike FRET.

In this study, a new type of NCDs is developed by a microwave reaction. Under optimal excitation, the prepared NCDs exhibit blue PL emission. The structure of NCDs is identified by various characterizations. Subsequently, a PL system is proposed for hematin determination. The results show a good linear relationship between 0.2 and 13.0 μmol/L range, with LOD of 0.07 μmol/L. The quenching mechanism is confirmed to be caused by IFE. Moreover, the PL system is found to be highly effective in quantitatively detecting the hematin content in hemolysate samples. As a result, this system is regarded as a cost-effective, highly sensitive, and precise method of sensing. It has the potential to offer a novel hematin nanoprobe for disease diagnosis and trace recognition, thus holding promise for advancements in medical diagnostics.

Tianze Li: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Software, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yuanyuan Dong: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration. Qiuyan Yan: Visualization, Validation, Investigation. Zixin Liu: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Hong Zeng: Methodology, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Heilongjiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (LH2023E113), National Nature Foundation Cultivation Project of the Fundamental Scientific Research Funds for Heilongjiang Provincial Universities (2024GJ02), and the National College Students’ Innovative Entrepreneurial Training Plan Program (202411802100X). The authors would like to thank Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the support of materials characterizations. The authors also would like to thank their colleagues and other students who participated in this study.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2024.135634.
